items.title: WEC. "Il team ha fatto un miracolo": BMW trionfa alla 6 Ore di Spa, festa McLaren e beffa Ferrari in LMGT3
items.description: Una doppietta BMW che profuma di storia alle Ardenne: dopo 27 anni i bavaresi tornano a dominare l'Endurance. In LMGT3 McLaren vince in rimonta approf
items.body:
Per inviarci segnalazioni, foto e video puoi contattarci su info@moto.it

Le Ardenne non perdonano, ma sanno anche premiare l'audacia. Davanti a una marea umana di oltre 100.000 spettatori, la 6 Ore di Spa-Francorchamps 2026 ha scritto una pagina di storia destinata a restare negli annali del motorsport: la prima vittoria assoluta di BMW nel FIA WEC, impreziosita da una doppietta che mancava ai bavaresi dai tempi d'oro della V12 LMR a Le Mans nel 1999.

Partite dalla quinta fila, le BMW M Hybrid V8 del team WRT hanno giocato d'azzardo fin dal primo giro. In una gara frammentata da ben quattro Safety Car, il muretto belga guidato da Vincent Vosse ha estratto il coniglio dal cilindro già al termine della prima ora: uno short-fueling aggressivo che ha proiettato la numero 20 di René Rast al comando. Rast, in stato di grazia, ha martellato giri veloci uno dopo l'altro, consegnando poi il testimone a Sheldon van der Linde e Robin Frijns. In aria pulita, la LMDh tedesca ha dimostrato una costanza di rendimento spaventosa, gestendo il distacco mentre alle sue spalle infuriava la battaglia. "È una storia incredibile per noi", ha commentato entusiasta Rast a fine gara. "Il team ha compiuto un miracolo tattico. Mi hanno messo davanti e l’auto volava".

Se la #20 è stata la lepre, la numero 15 è stata il mastino. Kevin Magnussen ha messo in pista tutta la sua proverbiale aggressività nei duelli corpo a corpo. Sopravvissuto a ben due contatti con la Ferrari #51, il danese ha messo in atto una vera masterclass difensiva, chiudendo la porta in faccia a rivali del calibro di Antonio Fuoco e Kamui Kobayashi a bordo della Toyota. Magnussen ha difeso la seconda posizione con i denti, nonostante montasse gomme Michelin decisamente più affaticate rispetto agli inseguitori, permettendo al marchio bavarese di festeggiare un uno-due che profuma di rivincita. La Ferrari 499P #50 di Fuoco, Nielsen e Molina è riuscita comunque ad agguantare il terzo gradino del podio, ma resta l'amaro in bocca per un problema a un dado ruota durante il secondo pit-stop che è costato 20 preziosissimi secondi. Ai piedi del podio brilla l'Aston Martin Valkyrie: Tom Gamble, con un sorpasso d'autore su Kobayashi a sei giri dalla fine, ha regalato al marchio inglese il miglior risultato di sempre nella classe regina.

Note di merito anche per Genesis: il nuovo prototipo GMR-001 ha chiuso ottavo nella Hypercar, centrando i primi punti mondiali alla sua seconda apparizione. Gara da dimenticare, invece, per i costruttori francesi: la Peugeot, dopo la storica pole, è stata vittima di incidenti altrui, mentre l'Alpine #35 è uscita di scena con un violento schianto di Félix da Costa. Adesso, gli occhi sono tutti puntati sulla Sarthe. Tra poco più di un mese c'è la 24 Ore di Le Mans, e il paddock ci arriva con gerarchie totalmente stravolte: BMW è capolista, la Ferrari insegue (la #83 di AF Corse solo ottava e la #51 terzultima a Spa) e Porsche attende il grande salto.

Le emozioni forti non sono mancate in classe LMGT3 che ha vissuto un finale da thriller cinematografico. A trionfare è stata la McLaren 720S GT3 Evo #10 di Garage 59, autrice di una rimonta clamorosa dalla quindicesima casella in griglia. Il trio composto da Antares Au, Tom Fleming e Marvin Kirchhöfer ha trasformato la delusione del ritiro di Imola in pura estasi belga, risalendo la china grazie a una gestione gomme impeccabile e a un ritmo forsennato di Fleming nel cuore della gara. Il verdetto è arrivato però solo sotto la bandiera a scacchi: la Ferrari 296 GT3 di AF Corse, che aveva tagliato il traguardo per prima con un monumentale Alessio Rovera, è stata declassata al quarto posto a causa di una penalità di cinque secondi per un unsafe release durante l'ultimo stop.

Un errore fatale che ha consegnato il successo a McLaren e ha permesso all'Aston Martin di Mattia Drudi e alla Porsche di Manthey PureRxcing — autrice di un recupero prodigioso dopo un contatto iniziale con una Cadillac — di salire sul podio. Resta il rammarico per le Ford Mustang di Proton Competition, protagoniste di una prima fase d'attacco con il sorpasso mozzafiato di Powell all'Eau Rouge, prima di naufragare tra testacoda e penalità per track limits, lasciando a Stefano Gattuso solo la magra consolazione del Goodyear Wingfoot Award.

24 Ore di Le Mans 2024

WEC

Le luci al neon di Miami non sono mai state così brillanti, né l’atmosfera così densa di nostalgia edonistica. In un tributo che fonde la leggenda della Formula 1 con l’iconografia più pura degli anni ’80, Lewis Hamilton ha deciso di trasformare le strade della Florida nel set di un moderno “Miami Vice”. Non è solo un video, è un viaggio sensoriale che mette al centro una delle supercar più riconoscibili di sempre: la Ferrari Testarossa.

Tra Enzo e Hollywood

Per capire la portata del gesto di Hamilton, bisogna tornare indietro nel tempo, a quella controversia che vide protagonista Enzo Ferrari in persona. Nelle prime stagioni del celebre telefilm, Sonny Crockett e Rico Tubbs sfrecciavano su una Ferrari Daytona Spider che, in realtà, era una replica costruita su base Chevrolet Corvette. Quando il “Drake” lo scoprì, si indispettì a tal punto da pretendere il ritiro della finta Rossa (che fu fatta esplodere in un episodio) e inviò alla produzione una Testarossa bianca autentica. Quell’auto, con un solo specchietto e cerchi monodado, divenne istantaneamente un mito mondiale.

La scena incredibile

Hamilton, reduce da una simile impresa notturna a Tokyo al volante di una leggendaria F40, ha voluto alzare l’asticella una volta arrivato a “Vice City”. La narrazione inizia sull’acqua: il sette volte campione del mondo appare inizialmente ai comandi di un Wellcraft 38kv Scarab, lo stesso iconico motoscafo guidato da Don Johnson nella serie originale.
Ma è una volta toccata terra che la magia si compie. Vestito di bianco dalla testa ai piedi, Hamilton impugna il volante di una Testarossa bianca immacolata. Non si tratta della versione esatta della serie, ma di un esemplare più recente, riconoscibile dai due specchietti retrovisori e dai cerchi a cinque dadi.

Un contrasto di stili

La scena che segue è pura estetica visiva. Sotto una palette cromatica pastello che richiama i tramonti e le atmosfere della Florida di quarant’anni fa, Hamilton scatena la potenza del dodici cilindri in una serie di traversi spettacolari per le vie di Miami.
Tuttavia, il pilota ha voluto inserire un elemento di rottura personale, una licenza poetica che allontana il video dai canoni classici del pop e della synthwave anni ’80. Al posto delle hit del telefilm, il rombo del motore Ferrari è accompagnato dalle note crude del gangsta rap degli N.W.A.. È l’incontro definitivo tra due epoche: il lusso intramontabile della Ferrari e l’attitudine urbana e ribelle di uno dei più grandi piloti della storia.
Vederlo scivolare tra le curve, avvolto in quella nuvola di fumo e stile, è la prova che certi miti non invecchiano mai; cambiano solo colonna sonora.

Visualizza questo post su Instagram

Un post condiviso da Lewis Hamilton (@lewishamilton)

items.title: “L’hantavirus ha fino a 50 giorni di incubazione, i sintomi sono molto vaghi all’inizio. Non ci sono cure e non c’è neanche un vaccino”: così Burioni a Che Tempo che Fa
items.description: “I sintomi sono molto vaghi all’inizio: febbre e difficoltà respiratorie, però poi diventa una malattia molto grave con una mortalità supe
items.body:
Trending News
        
    
    
    
            “L’hantavirus ha fino a 50 giorni di incubazione, i sintomi sono molto vaghi all’inizio. Non ci sono cure e non c’è neanche un vaccino”: così Burioni a Che Tempo che Fa
    
            A "Che tempo che fa", il virologo del San Raffaele analizza l'emergenza scaturita dalla crociera sbarcata a Tenerife. Sotto sorveglianza anche 4 italiani
    
    
            
            di Redazione FqMagazine

11 Maggio 2026

Commenti

Segui FQ su

WhatsApp

Discover

“I sintomi sono molto vaghi all’inizio: febbre e difficoltà respiratorie, però poi diventa una malattia molto grave con una mortalità superiore al 30%. Non ci sono cure e non c’è neanche un vaccino. O meglio, c’è ma hanno deciso di non svilupparlo perché non avevo un mercato sufficiente, è sempre il solito discorso”. È con queste parole che il professor Roberto Burioni ha inquadrato l’emergenza hantavirus. Ospite di Fabio Fazio nel salotto di “Che tempo che fa” sul Nove, il virologo dell’Università Vita-Salute San Raffaele ha tracciato il quadro clinico ed epidemiologico del focolaio esploso a bordo della nave da crociera recentemente sbarcata a Tenerife, in Spagna. Una situazione che tocca da vicino anche l’Italia: le autorità sanitarie hanno infatti posto sotto sorveglianza quattro connazionali, rientrati a casa a bordo di un volo su cui, durante un breve scalo in Sudafrica, era salita per pochi minuti una donna successivamente deceduta proprio a causa del virus.
Il punto della situazione
L’analisi del virologo parte dai numeri e dalle evidenze scientifiche attualmente disponibili, invitando alla prudenza. “Questo virus non è molto contagioso per gli uomini. Però, c’è un però…”, ha esordito Burioni, spiegando i limiti della letteratura medica in materia. “Quello che sappiamo si basa sull’osservazione di episodi epidemici molto piccoli, il più grande è di 31 casi. Quindi, quello che sappiamo è relativamente poco”. Sulla base di questi dati, l’esperto ha circoscritto la portata dell’allarme, evidenziando tuttavia la vera insidia di questo patogeno, legata ai tempi di latenza dell’infezione: “Per quello che noi sappiamo, non siamo di fronte a una nuova epidemia. Però, c’è un però… Questa malattia ha un’incubazione molto lunga, che arriva fino a 50 giorni, dobbiamo sperare che nei prossimi 50 giorni nessuno diventi positivo”.
L’isolamento obbligatorio e il rischio mutazioni
Proprio l’assenza di terapie mirate e di una copertura vaccinale (bloccata, come sottolineato in apertura, da esclusive logiche di mercato) rende il tracciamento dei contatti e il rigido isolamento le uniche armi efficaci per spegnere il focolaio sul nascere. Il protocollo di sorveglianza attuato per i passeggeri della crociera e per i loro contatti – inclusi i quattro italiani – risponde a una precisa necessità di contenimento preventivo. “Però diciamo che adesso queste persone devono essere isolate, perché i virus possono anche cambiare: quindi un virus che non è contagioso può diventarlo”, ha avvertito chiaramente Burioni. Al momento, la diffusione del patogeno risulta comunque sotto controllo e confinata a una cerchia specifica di individui. “Però, in questo momento, il pericolo del contagio è ristretto a quelli che erano nella nave e quelli che hanno interagito con loro”, ha rassicurato il professore. Il conto alla rovescia per dichiarare superata la fase critica è dunque dettato unicamente dall’orologio biologico del virus: “Deve esserci una quarantena di una cinquantina di giorni, se tra 50 giorni non c’è stato, come spero, nessun positivo al di fuori di quella della nave, possiamo tirare un sospiro di sollievo”.

Che tempo che faFabio FazioRoberto BurioniVirus

items.title: Werner Herzog rifiuta Cannes: Bucking Fastard non era in Concorso. Ma lo sarà al Festiva di Venezia
items.description: Werner Herzog ha rifiutato il Festival di Cannes. Nelle scorse ore Variety ha riportato in esclusiva la notizia dell’assenza di Bucking Fastard, il nu
items.body:
Cinema
        
    
    
    
            Werner Herzog rifiuta Cannes: Bucking Fastard non era in Concorso. Ma lo sarà al Festiva di Venezia
    
            Il nuovo film del regista tedesco era stato inserito nella “Selezione ufficiale”, ma non tra i titoli in corsa per la Palma d’Oro. La produzione ha declinato l’invito. Un caso che ricorda il precedente di Jim Jarmusch
    
    
            
            di Davide Turrini

11 Maggio 2026

Commenti

Segui FQ su

WhatsApp

Discover

Werner Herzog ha rifiutato il Festival di Cannes. Nelle scorse ore Variety ha riportato in esclusiva la notizia dell’assenza di Bucking Fastard, il nuovo film di fiction dell’83enne cineasta tedesco, nel programma della Croisette di quest’anno. Il motivo? Frémaux e soci non hanno ritenuto il film abbastanza valido per il Concorso. Come si sa, nei festival di rango, tra cui anche Venezia e Berlino, esiste una formula allargata per inserire più titoli possibili in primo piano. Si tratta dell’invito nella “Selezione ufficiale” che, in pratica, vuol dire tutto e non vuol dire niente. Si può essere nella selezione ufficiale sia quando si è in Concorso – e gareggiare per la Palma d’Oro – sia in una categoria minore (Certain Regard) o addirittura come proiezione speciale o fuori concorso. Ebbene, secondo Variety, a Herzog non è stato offerto il Concorso, quindi l’invito ufficiale cannense, nell’ampia “selezione ufficiale”, l’autore di Fitzcarraldo l’avrà usato per spolverare il mobilio di casa. Un portavoce della produzione di Bucking Fastard ha spiegato che l’invito è “stato gentilmente declinato”. Herzog, riporta sempre Variety, sperava che la presenza nel cast delle attrici protagoniste Kate e Rooney Mara – pare in grande spolvero nella parte di due sorelle gemelle – offrisse al film un appeal ulteriore in termini di cast internazionale e hollywoodiano, per concorrere a qualche premio.
Il fatto che inizialmente Cannes avesse comunque annunciato Bucking Fastard nella “selezione ufficiale” ha creato ulteriore confusione. Si tratta comunque del gran rifiuto di un nome importante del cinema mondiale, nonostante le sue produzioni non riescano più ad avere la magia dei suoi film degli anni Ottanta. Va poi aggiunto che a Cannes si sta cercando di far mutare pelle da anni al Concorso, scostando qualche grosso storico nome per farcene entrare di nuovi e meno conosciuti. Anche se l’operazione del rinnovamento autoriale è diventata una faccenda complessa e sempre più di nicchia proprio perché le giovani firme che Cannes coltivò tra i Settanta e gli Ottanta – pensiamo solo a Francis Ford Coppola – non esplodono più con la forza commerciale e la spinta della critica dell’epoca.
Per il famoso, e terrificante, Megalopolis nel 2024, il tentennamento cannense fu altrettanto conclamato. Alla fine il film di Coppola, si dice su grossa pressione del regista stesso, venne inserito nel Concorso, ma la scelta non si rivelò così fortunata. Il caso Herzog ricorda invece quello che è accaduto a Jim Jarmusch nel 2025 con Father Mother Sister Brother: Cannes lo invitò in selezione ufficiale ma non in Concorso e Jarmusch rifiutò. Il film finì, ovviamente, in Concorso a Venezia vincendo addirittura il Leone d’Oro, incassando quasi nove milioni di dollari nel mondo.
Su Indiewire, il regista di Daunbailò dichiarò che quando gli organizzatori francesi gli dissero che avrebbero voluto mettere Father Mother Sister Brother non in Concorso ma in un’altra sezione, lui rispose: “Non faccio film da cinque anni. Sono stato a Cannes molte volte e questa collocazione non è per me appropriata. Il Concorso a Cannes per me è fondamentale, non per la competizione in sé, ma per la visibilità che darà all’uscita del mio film in modo da poter ripagare gli investitori e poter realizzare un altro film. Non è una questione di premi”. Aggiungiamo anche che finire in altre sezioni che non siano quella del Concorso di Cannes non è affatto disonorevole (Marco Bellocchio è stato presente tra fine anni Novanta e inizio secolo con molti suoi titoli nel Certain Regard), ma che alla fine gli slot temporali e l’attenzione dei media verso i film in Concorso sono più convenienti per la produzione di un film rispetto a sezioni cosiddette minori.
La sinossi ufficiale di Bucking Fastard racconta le vicende di Jeana e Joan Holbrooke (le sorelle Mara) che, alla ricerca di una terra immaginaria dove il vero amore è possibile, iniziano a scavare un tunnel attraverso una catena montuosa. Nel cast figurano anche Orlando Bloom e Domhnall Gleeson. Kate Mara, in una recente intervista, aveva elogiato Herzog e il suo lavoro: “Non c’è nessuno al mondo come lui. Lavorare con lui è stato magico e condividere l’esperienza con mia sorella è stato come realizzare un sogno”. Bucking Fastard sarà ovviamente il primo titolo già presente nel Concorso di Venezia 2026, dove Herzog nel 2025 ha ricevuto un Leone d’Oro alla carriera e ha presentato il documentario Ghost Elephants… Fuori Concorso.

Festival di CannesMostra del Cinema di Venezia 2025Werner Herzog

items.title: Biomethane Academy, iscrizioni aperte
items.description: Sono aperte le iscrizioni per partecipare ai corsi della Biomethane Academy. Si tratta della prima scuola italiana interamente dedicata alla formazion
items.body:
Biomethane Academy, iscrizioni aperte

11 Maggio 2026

Sono aperte le iscrizioni per partecipare ai corsi della Biomethane Academy. Si tratta della prima scuola italiana interamente dedicata alla formazione professionale nel settore del biometano, nata dalla collaborazione tra CIB-Consorzio Italiano Biogas e Cornucopia Società Cooperativa Sociale ETS – ente formativo accreditato presso Regione Lombardia.L’iniziativa nasce per rispondere alle crescenti esigenze formative del nostro settore che, grazie anche ai fondi PNRR, sta vivendo una fase di sviluppo e forte espansione.I corsi prenderanno il via dal mese di ottobre 2026 e si articoleranno su due livelli, sulla base dei due nuovi profili professionali ufficialmente riconosciuti dal Quadro Regionale degli Standard Professionali (QRSP) della regione Lombardia:Operatore di impianto (150 ore) Pensato per chi è già in possesso delle principali abilitazioni, include teoria e pratica su impianti in funzione, con un focus operativo avanzato. I corsi relativi a questo profilo si svolgeranno in modalità ibrida: formazione a distanza, in presenza o in campo.Manager di impianti (130 ore) Il più alto livello formativo, curato dai migliori esperti del settore, con focus su management, normativa, sostenibilità e innovazione. I corsi relativi a questo profilo si svolgeranno in modalità formazione a distanza.I percorsi in modalità ibrida saranno integrati da visite didattiche in impianti di eccellenza, che consentiranno ai partecipanti di osservare sul campo processi e tecnologie all’avanguardia.I percorsi formativi rilasciano certificazioni riconosciute in tutta l’Unione Europea, a garanzia della spendibilità delle competenze acquisite sia in ambito nazionale che internazionale.Le aziende interessate a iscrivere i propri dipendenti ai corsi della Biomethane Academy potranno accedere a fondi regionali per la formazione continua. I tecnici di Cornucopia Società Cooperativa Sociale ETS affiancheranno le imprese in ogni fase del percorso, dalla presentazione della domanda fino alla rendicontazione, offrendo così un servizio completo e personalizzato.Per informazioni e pre-iscrizioni è possibile contattare ​​​​​​​formazione@consorziobiogas.it | Tel. +39 0371 4662633 int. 806 – 810 Biomethane Academy è un modello di formazione all’avanguardia con l’obiettivo di costruire un ecosistema di professionalità qualificate a supporto della filiera agricola e della sostenibilità ambientale. La scuola rappresenta un progetto innovativo in un momento chiave per la transizione ecologica del Paese, che formerà competenze tecniche altamente specializzate, aggiornate e certificate.A garanzia della qualità: il Comitato Tecnico ScientificoLe attività della Biomethane Academy sono supervisionate da un Comitato Tecnico Scientifico (CTS), che annovera figure di primo piano del mondo scientifico e industriale:Angelo Baronchelli, imprenditore e innovatore nel settore delle energie rinnovabili, fondatore di Gruppo AB, Vice presidente del CIB;Valeria Comensoli, imprenditrice agricola bresciana dell’Azienda Agricola Malgherosse;Matteo Grandi, Country Manager di Verdalia Bioenergy in Italia;Federico Frascari, direttore tecnico di Biological Care, biologo esperto in gestione di impianti di biometanoAndrea Chiabrando, direttore tecnico del Consorzio Monviso Agroenergia, esperto di modelli integrati di economia circolare in agricoltura						
				
				
				
					
			
						
									VISITA IL SITO DI BIOMETHANE ACADEMY

items.title: Global patterns of urban heat shaped by climate and morphology
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 11 May 2026

Global patterns of urban heat shaped by climate and morphology
                    Siwoo Lee1 na1, Cheolhee Yoo 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-3922-23002,3 na1, Bokyung Son 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-8410-731X1, Dongjin Cho4, Jungho Im 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-4506-68771,5,6 & …T. C. Chakraborty 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-1338-35257 Show authors

Nature Communications

, Article number:  (2026)
            Cite this article
                    
                    
                    
                            We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its
                            findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note
                            there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply.

Subjects
        
            AttributionClimate-change impactsProjection and prediction

AbstractUrban morphology and background climate are evolving drivers of the urban heat island (UHI) effect, yet the thermal influence of surrounding urban structure and its coupling with climate remain elusive. Here, we show that climate and morphology jointly shape global urban heat. Using a six-class urban typology, long-term climatology, and machine learning, we quantify the thermal influence of surrounding urban structure and project future urban heat across 2,213 cities. We define a city-level thermal impact of the surrounding built environment (TBE) as the area-weighting UHI change induced by specific built-up types. Climatically, cold regions most frequently exhibit high daytime TBE, while arid regions dominate high nighttime TBE. Structurally, a universal pattern persists; high TBE corresponds to denser and taller forms, whereas sparser and lower types dominate low TBE during day and night. Future projections indicate that climate change dominates TBE change in 69% of cities, whereas the Global South exhibits stronger tendencies toward morphology-driven and synergistic intensification than the Global North. Our results highlight the need for locally tailored adaptation strategies that target the dominant drivers—climatic, morphological, or both.

items.title: Critical role of low cloud feedback in irreversible sea level rise
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 11 May 2026

Critical role of low cloud feedback in irreversible sea level rise
                    Sunhee Wang1, Yechul Shin 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-4381-697X1, Ji-Hoon Oh 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-8484-89972, Hanjun Kim 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-5005-86553 & …Jong-Seong Kug 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-2251-25791,4 Show authors

Nature Communications

, Article number:  (2026)
            Cite this article
                    
                    
                    
                            We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its
                            findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note
                            there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply.

Subjects
        
            Atmospheric dynamicsPhysical oceanographyProjection and prediction

AbstractDespite mitigation efforts, anthropogenic emissions are projected to cause a global mean sea level rise of approximately 0.44 m and sustained sea levels over human timescales (e.g., centuries). Even without glacial melting, sea level remains elevated for centuries due to the substantial thermal inertia of the ocean interior. However, mechanisms governing persistent sea level rise remain inadequately understood. Here, using a fully coupled climate model, we demonstrate that a distinct sea surface temperature pattern drives enhanced and prolonged sea level rise. This surface warming pattern enhances the absorption of shortwave radiation through climate feedbacks, such as low cloud and sea ice-albedo feedbacks, thereby sustaining ocean heat uptake, thermal expansion, and continued sea level rise. Atmosphere-only simulations confirm that the sea surface temperature pattern alone explains the increased shortwave radiation through the positive feedbacks. These findings underscore the importance of accurately representing surface warming patterns and associated cloud responses in future sea level rise projections.

items.title: From intermittent to persistent chill insufficiency in California’s specialty crops
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 09 May 2026

From intermittent to persistent chill insufficiency in California’s specialty crops
                    Prakash K. Jha 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-9268-32881, Geruo A 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-5714-402X2, Tapan B. Pathak1,3 & …Isabella Velicogna2,4 Show authors

Communications Sustainability

volume 1, Article number: 76 (2026)
            Cite this article

Subjects
        
            AgricultureClimate-change impacts

AbstractSufficient winter chill is critical for breaking dormancy and enabling normal flowering and fruit set in temperature fruit and nut crops. Here, we use observational data to show that increasing interannual temperature variability has already reduced winter chill in California’s Central Valley, contrary to previous projections that chill insufficiency would occur only by the mid-to-late 21st century. Inadequate chill is affecting crops such as pistachio and plum, particularly in Southern California. We evaluate adaptation strategies using subseasonal temperature forecasts to assess chill sufficiency by early February and guide dormancy-breaking treatments in low-chill years. Using this approach, we demonstrate that a simple in-season forecasting strategy, driven by subseasonal temperature forecasts, can predict chill accumulation within 10 percent of observed winter chill in 89–94 percent of cases. We also propose revised chill thresholds to inform breeding programs for low-chill varieties and guide management interventions to reduce risks in low-chill years.

                        IntroductionWinter chill accumulation, defined as the cumulative exposure to cool temperatures during dormancy, is a key regulator of phenological development in temperate fruit and nut trees1,2 and is commonly quantified as chill portions1,3 (CP) or chill hours4 (CH). These crops require sufficient chill to transition out of winter dormancy and initiate their growth cycle. Each crop has specific chill requirements for proper development, known as its chill threshold. For instance, satisfactory growth typically requires approximately 38–54 CP for walnuts (Juglans regia), 36–65 for pistachios (Pistacia vera), 30–70 for cherries (Prunus spp.), and 55–60 for plums (Prunus spp.), depending on the variety5. Because many commercial cultivars of these crops operate near their chilling thresholds, modest reductions in winter chill (e.g., 5–10 CP) can be sufficient to shift a season from adequate to insufficient chilling. Inadequate chill can disrupt winter dormancy release and subsequent phenological development, leading to delayed flowering, irregular bud break, prolonged bloom periods, and result in uneven fruit development and ripening6. These disruptions reduce fruit quality, extend harvest periods, and lead to substantial yield losses. Conversely, when the chill requirements are fulfilled too early and are followed by unseasonably warm temperatures, bud break may occur prematurely, increasing the vulnerability of tender buds to spring frost damage7. Given these challenges, accurate chill prediction is critical for planning management interventions, such as the use of dormancy-breaking agents to promote uniform bud break under insufficient chill and frost protection strategies. Here, we evaluate the potential of subseasonal to seasonal forecasts (S2S) to provide chill sufficiency information with lead times of up to one season in advance.Concerns have intensified over declining chill accumulation in regions with Mediterranean-type climates characterized by relatively mild winters, such as California. Unlike cooler temperate regions, California operates within a narrow margin, where the difference between the required and available chill is small, leaving its specialty crops vulnerable to even a modest increase in temperature8. Several studies have documented this decline in chill. For example, Baldocchi and Wong8 estimated a loss of ~40 CH per decade. Luedeling et al9. projected that winter chill in California’s Central Valley could decline by 30–60% by mid-century and up to 80% by the end of the century, relative to the 700–1200 CH observed around 1950. Such reductions could push many regions below the physiological thresholds needed for successful bud break, thereby reducing flowering and fruit set10. These studies robustly document long-term declines in winter chill but largely emphasize mean trends rather than year-to-year variability.Crucially, climate change is not only causing gradual warming but also amplifying the interannual variability of temperature11,12,13, a trend often overlooked in agriculture impact studies. This dual challenge—declining mean and rising variability in chill accumulation—narrows the buffer that historically protected growers from occasional low-chill years. In the past, when available chill exceeded crop requirements by a wide margin, minor forecasting errors had relatively limited consequences for crops needing a high amount of winter chill. Today, however, the margin is shrinking, and unreliable forecasts may result in costly management missteps.Despite extensive documentation of long-term declines in winter chill9,14,15, two critical gaps remain. First, most prior studies focus on mean trends, leaving year-to-year variability largely unquantified, even though it directly determines how frequently chill requirements are not met. Second, it is unclear whether widely used Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP) climate models capture not only the magnitude of observed chill declines but also their interannual variability. Addressing these gaps is essential for distinguishing long-term climate-driven chill risk from operational risk and for evaluating the reliability of climate information used in agricultural decision-making. These gaps motivate the present study, which integrates observed variability, climate projections, and S2S forecasts to assess winter chill risk across decision-relevant timescales.Year-to-year chill variability is often governed by minimum daily temperatures during the coldest portions of the diurnal cycle, so monthly averages may obscure short-lived cold extremes critical for crop physiology. For example, Jha and Pathak16 used monthly-averaged forecasts to evaluate chill variability, but such approaches may inadequately capture these extremes. This highlights the need to evaluate chill accumulation using daily-resolution observations and forecasts.Understanding long-term trends and interannual variability in winter chill is especially important for perennial crops, which require several years to reach full productivity and remain in production for decades. Aligning orchard lifespans and breeding timelines with projected changes in chill underscores the importance of targeting appropriate chill requirements in new cultivars, motivating the need for reliable projections of future chill conditions. Innovative approaches such as Speed Breeding, which uses controlled environmental conditions to accelerate breeding cycles, can shorten breeding timelines and support faster development of climate-adapted cultivars, particularly when integrated with marker-assisted selection and genomic tools17.More generally, adaptation strategies that integrate climate information with crop phenology and management can help mitigate climate change impacts on perennial crops18.In this study, we analyze 44 years of observed daily temperatures from GRIDMET (hereafter referred to as the observational data) to quantify both long-term trend and interannual variability in winter chill accumulation across major fruit and nut-producing regions in California. We evaluate whether current conditions are already approaching or crossing crop-specific chill thresholds by comparing the observed trends and interannual variability in winter chill with CMIP6 projections under a high-emissions pathway. This comparison allows us to assess whether the magnitude and interannual variability of observed declines are consistent with projections under an upper-bound warming scenario and whether the earliest emergence of chill-related risk aligns with timelines reported in previous studies.To assess whether state-of-the-art forecast systems can help growers prepare for the risk of chill deficit, we evaluate the performance of S2S forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF). Specifically, we examine how well the forecasts capture interannual variability in winter chill accumulation at both monthly and daily temporal resolutions, and how accurately they identify years with insufficient chill.Together, these analyses go beyond long-term trends to quantify how increasing interannual variability alters the frequency of years falling below critical chill thresholds, thereby complementing long-term climate projections by revealing near-term chill-related risk. The study focuses on high-value fruit and nut crops for which California is a leading U.S. producer, including pistachios, walnuts, plums, and cherries, all of which require substantial winter chill for optimal development19. These crops are not only critical to California’s agricultural economy but also play a very important role in national and global food supply chains.ResultsObserved trends in chill accumulationAnalysis of the observational data reveals a notable decline in chill accumulation across major crop-growing regions in California over recent decades. During the baseline period of 1980–1990, average CP in southern California (below 37° latitude) was approximately 64 CP in pistachio and cherry-growing regions, and 65 in walnut and plum regions, with interannual variability reflected by standard deviations (SDs) of 4–4.5 CP and 95% confidence intervals ranging from about 61 to 68 CP across regions. Over the most recent decade (2015–2024), these values declined to roughly 60 CP in all four crop regions, with higher interannual variability (SDs of 6–6.4 CP, 95% CI: ~55–65 CP), representing a decrease of about 4–6 CPs relative to the baseline period (Figs. 1 and 2; and Supplementary Figs. 1 and 2). Linear trend analysis indicates a statistically significant downward trajectory in chill accumulation across all four crop-growing regions (Fig. 2), reflecting the influence of long-term warming. Based on crop-specific chill thresholds, calculated as the median of CP across all listed varieties by Pope5 (58 for pistachios, 47.5 for walnuts, 48 for cherries, and 57.5 for plums), the current trend suggests that many regions are approaching the minimum required chill thresholds for these crops. This poses a concern as full yield potential with quality fruit for these crops cannot be realized without sufficient chill accumulation.Fig. 1: Trends in winter chill changes across California from 1980 to 2023.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe map shows the rate of change (slope) in winter chill over the period 1980–2023 at each grid point, estimated using the Theil–Sen estimator. Future projections are based on the SSP 5-8.5 Shared Socioeconomic Pathway.Spatially, the decline in chill accumulation over the period 1980–2024 is widespread across all four crop-growing regions in California, with 91%–95% of grid cells within each crop region showing negative trends in chill accumulation over time, based on Sen’s Slope Estimator20. Statistical significance of these trends was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted Mann–Kendell test21,22. The proportion of grid cells showing significant declines (p < 0.05) in CP is 50% in pistachio regions, 57% in walnut regions, 53% in cherry regions, and 54% in plum regions.Rising interannual variabilityImportantly, here we find the emergence of a higher interannual variability regime in chill accumulation since the late 1990s, alongside long-term declines in mean chill. This is reflected in the 10-year moving SD of chill accumulation, which is substantially higher in recent decades than in the 1990s (Fig. 3) and remains persistently elevated thereafter. Although variability appears relatively stable after ~1998, the median rolling SD increased from approximately 4.3–4.6 during the 1990s to 7.1–7.2 in the post-1998 periods across all crops (Mann–Whitney test, p < 0.01), corresponding to an increase of about 56%. This dual pattern of declining means and increasing variability increases the likelihood of years falling below the physiological thresholds required for optimal development of these crops. While linear trend-based trajectories suggest that median chilling thresholds may not be consistently crossed until later decades, the proximity of current chill availability to these thresholds already implies heightened vulnerability to episodic chill insufficiency under present-day climate conditions. Such risk has indeed materialized: inadequate chill accumulation (i.e., with CP falling below its critical median threshold) was observed in pistachio, cherry, and plum-growing regions during only 3, 0, and 2 years, respectively, in the first half of the observational record (1979–1998). In contrast, these numbers increased to 6, 1, and 5 years, respectively, during the latter half of the record (2004–2023; Fig. 2). Although inadequate chill has not been observed for walnuts, due to their relatively low chill requirement, years with chill accumulation just 5 CPs above threshold occurred only once before 1998, but the frequency has tripled in the years since. These results indicate that chill disruptions associated with low chill accumulations are already occurring due to increasing interannual variability and are becoming more frequent across California, particularly for pistachio and plum-growing regions, which experienced insufficient chill in nearly 30% and 25% of the years, respectively, over the past two decades, even though long-term trend-based threshold crossings are projected to occur later. Together, these results highlight that changes in variability play a critical role in determining when and how frequently chill insufficiency occurs and, therefore, must be considered alongside mean trends when evaluating climate change impacts on perennial cropping systems.Fig. 3: Increasing interannual variability in CP in crop-growing regions of California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows changes in interannual variability of CP for crop-growing regions south of 37° latitude in California, based on GRIDMET data. At each time step, the SD of CP was computed across all grid points within the region using a 10-year moving window.Climate model simulations underestimating the riskWe evaluate whether long-term changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency risk are captured in the climate simulations from the latest generation of Climate Model Intercomparison Project, phase 6 (CMIP6). Because chill accumulation within a given year is strongly influenced by weather variability, our analysis focuses on changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency rather than predicting exact within-year outcomes. Across all four crop-growing regions, CMIP6 historical simulations (1981–2014) substantially underestimate interannual variability in chill accumulation, with the models’ SD roughly half that of observations (about 53% lower). As a result, CMIP6 models fail to reproduce a large fraction of observed chill insufficiency events at annual timescales when evaluated against median cultivar-specific chill requirements. In the historical period, CMIP6 captures only 0–2 insufficient chill years across regions, compared to 2–6 observed events depending on crop, indicating that roughly 60–70% of insufficiency occurrences are missed (Fig. 2). Because the median chill thresholds of walnut and cherry are lower than for pistachio and plum, historical chill insufficiency has been rare, occurring only once in cherry and not at all in walnut during the historical period.Climate projections under SSP5-8.5 starting in 2015 indicate a marked increase in chill insufficiency risk. Pistachio and plum-growing regions experience frequent insufficiency, occurring in 18–19 of the 26 years between 2015 and 2040, whereas walnut and cherry regions begin to show occasional insufficiency (one year by 2039), suggesting that these traditionally low-risk crops may transition toward more frequent chill insufficiency in the early 2040s or beyond if current trends persist (Fig. 2). Importantly, by analyzing GRIDMET observations, we find that these thresholds are already being crossed intermittently for pistachio, cherry, and plum under present-day climate variability, although trend-based analysis indicates that the transition to persistent chill insufficiency occurs later than indicated by isolated low-chill years (Fig. 2). This discrepancy arises because chill accumulation depends on short-term variability, which is not fully represented in CMIP6 model simulations when analyzed at annual timescale. Since CMIP6 models underestimate the observed interannual variability in chill accumulation, projections based solely on mean trend threshold crossings likely underrepresent the near-term frequency of insufficient chill years, particularly in walnut growing regions. Prior works based on climate projections have suggested that the ongoing warming will significantly reduce the accumulation of winter chill in California by mid- to end-century, with conditions expected to no longer support some of the key tree crops currently grown in the region9.Our results refine this interpretation by distinguishing between episodic chill insufficiency that is already occurring due to interannual variability and the longer-term shift toward persistent insufficiency indicated by declining trends. Together, these findings indicate that mid- to late-century timelines may underestimate the urgency of the problem, as physiologically disruptive declines in winter chill are already emerging prior to mid-century and are projected to intensify under continued warming, even if trend-based thresholds are crossed later in the century.We further investigate whether these trends are consistent across administrative boundaries. Analysis at the county level reveals a broadly similar pattern: insufficient chill accumulation has become more frequent across most counties over the past two decades (Supplementary Fig. 5).The finding has important implications for both agricultural adaptation and the use of climate model projections for agricultural risk assessment. First, it indicates that increased interannual variability can lead to earlier occurrences of chill insufficiency than would be expected from long-term mean trends alone. As a result, growers may need to implement adaptation strategies earlier than suggested by mean climate projections. These may include selecting low chill cultivars, applying dormancy-breaking agents, or considering shifts in production regions to maintain crop viability under a warming and more variable climate. Second, this suggests that CMIP6-based projections may underestimate near-term chill risk if variability at the colder end of the temperature distribution (e.g., below 7 °C) is not adequately captured.Evaluation of ECMWF’s forecast skill for chill predictionOur analysis shows that 1-month lead forecasts (NDJ observations + February forecasts) are highly accurate, with predicted chill in the four crop-growing regions within ±10% of observed values for 42 of 43 years using ECMWF daily forecasts (MD) and 41 of 43 years using monthly mean forecasts (MM) (Supplementary Fig. S6). Applying a stricter ±5% threshold reduces the number of accurate years to 30 (MD) and 28 (MM). Grid-level evaluation followed a similar pattern, with 89–91% of grids within ±10%, dropping to 57–60% within ±5%. For 2-month lead forecasts (ND observations + January–February forecasts), accuracy declined to 39 (MD) and 35 (MM) years within ±10%, and only 26 (MD) and 23 (MM) years within ±5%. Longer lead times (3 and 4 months) further reduce performance at both regional and grid levels, with grid-level accuracy dropping from 55–73% (±10%) to 31–44% (±5%).The time series comparison of ECMWF hindcasts and GRIDMET-derived CP illustrates year-to-year differences between predicted and observed values (Supplementary Figs. 7 and 8). It is possible to observe how 1-month lead forecasts closely track observed winter (November–December–January–February (NDJF)), CP across all regions, while forecast skill decreases with longer lead times.The anomaly correlation coefficient (ACC) between NDJF total CP—computed as the ensemble mean of daily forecasts from individual ECMWF members, initialized on 1 November (lead time extending to 4 months)—and corresponding estimates from the observed temperature data ranged from 0.54 to 0.57 across the four crop-growing regions. When using monthly ECMWF forecasts, with CP similarly averaged across all ensemble members, the ACC values are even lower, ranging from 0.48 to 0.52. These relatively modest correlations indicate that the ECMWF forecasting system has limited skill in capturing the interannual variability in chill for these crop-growing regions in California. We also computed ACC between monthly total chill from the ECMWF temperature forecasts and the observed data for lead months 1–4 (November to February), separately (Fig. 4). In the four crop-growing regions (pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum), 94–95% of locations show ACC > 0.5 at the one-month lead (November), but this drops to only 1–3% in two-month lead (December), 0–7% in three-month lead (January), and to zero thereafter, indicating no usable skill beyond one month.Fig. 4: ACC between the total winter chill derived from the ECMWF forecast and observed temperature.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows the ACC between the monthly total chill derived from ECMWF forecasts and observed temperatures for November through February. Only regions common to pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum are displayed.The limited gains in skill despite leveraging both increased temporal granularity and ensemble averaging suggest that improving forecasts of chill will likely require more than just higher temporal resolution or ensemble spread. One possible explanation for the limited skill is that dynamic forecasting systems often rely heavily on ENSO as a source of predictability, even though ENSO has minimal direct influence on subseasonal climate variability in California23,24. Even in strong ENSO years, California’s climate anomalies often deviate substantially from expected patterns. We analyze the correlation between winter chill accumulation in four crop-growing regions and the Oceanic Niño Index (ONI) at various lead and lag intervals. Our results showed that chill accumulation during the NDJF period exhibits a weak positive correlation with ONI values from the next year’s December–March period (correlation coefficients ranging from 0.16 to 0.24), and a weak negative correlation with ONI values from the subsequent September-December period (correlation coefficients ranging from −0.20 to −0.26). In contrast, correlations with ONI values from the concurrent or previous year are generally weaker. We note that nonlinear ENSO effects, ENSO Modoki events, and multi-index predictors may also influence subseasonal climate variability in California, representing promising avenues for improving S2S forecast skill in future work.DiscussionTackling the sooner-than-expected decline in winter chillUnderstanding the implications of a sooner-than-expected decline in winter chill for the future cultivation of these crops in California is crucial. Addressing this challenge requires first understanding how reduced chill accumulation is impacting crop performance, how growers are currently responding, and evaluating the potential effectiveness of available adaptation strategies. Our study contributes to that effort by offering insights that can inform and enhance ongoing work in this area.In particular, our findings suggest that focusing solely on long-term mean declines may underestimate near-term agricultural risk. By showing that increasing interannual variability is already increasing the frequency of insufficient chill years, this study highlights a pathway through which chill insufficiency is emerging earlier than indicated by mean-based projections alone.Current varieties of major tree crops have limited tolerance for winter chill deficiency, with evidence suggesting they may endure up to about a 20 percent decline25. Historically, unusually warm winters, particularly in 1998 and 2013–2014, when CP dropped by about 25%, have already proved to exert significant impacts on crop production and revenue losses in the Central Valley25. As a result, cultivar innovation has emerged as a key adaptation strategy, particularly for high-chill crops. In cherries, experiments are underway to assess the sensitivity of existing cultivars such as Bings, Corals, Royal Hazel, Royal Lynn, Rainier, and Black Pearl located in the southern San Joaquin Valley to declining winter chill26. At the same time, lower-chill varieties such as Royal Lee, Minnie Royal, and Cristobalina are increasingly adopted in warmer regions, especially in Southern California27,28. Pistachio breeding efforts have produced cultivars like Golden Hills and Lost Hills with slightly reduced chill needs, inferred from the earlier bloom dates, but this has not been definitively established (UCCE Farm Advisor Phoebe E Gordon, personal communication). Walnut adaptation via cultivar replacement is slower, likely reflecting the historically low occurrence of chill insufficiency. This slower pace of adaptation is also reflected in the fact that cultivar selection in walnut has been primarily driven by yield, nut quality, and disease avoidance traits, with established cultivars such as Chandler remaining dominant in California (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean).Perennial orchard systems operate on multi-decadal timescales, with trees typically requiring several years to reach full production and remaining productive for decades. For example, pistachio, walnut, plum, and cherry trees reach peak production after approximately 7–25 years, while orchard lifespan often extends several decades, depending on the crop29,30,31,32,33. In parallel, the development of new cultivars requires 15–20 years when multiple breeding cycles are involved34. Given these multi-decadal timelines, cultivars initiated today would likely reach commercial production around 2040–2050, when chill accumulation is projected to average approximately 50 CP in these crop-growing regions. Breeding efforts should therefore prioritize perennial varieties' resilience to such low-chill and increasingly variable conditions.While chill thresholds are used here as indicators of dormancy fulfillment and risk, it is important to recognize that chilling and subsequent heat accumulation (forcing) interact in determining phenological development, with a well-established tradeoff between chilling and forcing requirements35,36. Incorporating this interaction would provide a more comprehensive representation of phenological responses, but it is beyond the scope of the present study.Despite this limitation, increasing interannual variability and the resulting uncertainty in chill accumulation highlight the need for actionable, in-season decision-support tools. In-season chill forecasts can offer an additional short-term adaptation tool, enabling growers to anticipate chill shortfalls and plan for the application of rest-breaking agents (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean). In California, cherry growers have already been using chill forecast tools, which work better for deciding when to apply dormancy-breaking agents9. Experiments are also ongoing to understand the effectiveness of rest-breaking agents for walnuts in different orchards under low-chill years37. For pistachios, chill forecasts are occasionally used to assess male–female bloom synchronization risks.Under low chill conditions, dormancy-breaking treatments typically begin by at least a month before the bud break37, often by the end of January. This operational timeline makes forecasts with at least a 1-month lead time essential for decision-making. Our results indicate that a practical approach for in-season chill assessment is to combine observed chill accumulation through January with 1-month ECMWF forecasts for February. This hybrid approach leverages the higher skill of short-lead forecasts (e.g., higher ACC and lower RMSE) while retaining observed variability earlier in the season. Using this method, NDJF chill totals were within 10% of observed values in nearly all years (41–42 years out of 43), providing a reliable basis for forecasting chill conditions. Although longer-lead forecasts are desirable for operational decisions, their skill is currently limited; therefore, this 1-month lead hybrid approach offers a near-term, operationally feasible solution for growers.The chill modeling strategy we present here, forecasting chill one month in advance, allows growers to adaptively manage chill shortfalls and make informed decisions on rest-breaking treatments and other nuanced orchard management practices. For instance, delaying pruning can help avoid stimulating premature bud break in years with low chill accumulation38. Similarly, forecasts can inform irrigation scheduling and orchard floor management strategies aimed at modulating microclimates39,40. While some cherry growers have adopted microclimate-modifying practices such as shade netting (e.g., DayChill) to enhance chill accumulation, similar applications remain limited for walnuts and pistachios26,41. Finally, the updated information on long-term chill decline in the future from this study can support broader strategic planning, such as the potential relocation of orchards. These projections make it clear that relocation decisions should not be viewed as a distant future concern. These management and adaptation options are presented to illustrate the range of responses to declining winter chill, a quantitative comparison of their costs, feasibility, or long-term effectiveness is beyond the scope of this study.MethodsStudy areaOur analysis focuses on regions across California where walnuts, pistachios, cherries, and plums are cultivated (Fig. 5). We assessed chill sufficiency separately for each crop, specific to its growing areas. To delineate these areas, we used the 2022 Cropland Data Layer (CDL) from USDA-NASS, which provides land use data at a 30 m resolution42. Crop-Specific masks were created by aggregating the CDL data to align with the 4 km resolution of the observational data. Chill sufficiency computation was then generated for each masked region at this 4 km resolution. Additionally, we emphasized crop-growing regions in southern California (south of 37° latitude), where previous studies have reported chill insufficiency risks16,25. The six counties with the largest crop-growing area for each crop are highlighted with bold blue outlines.DataDaily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures from the observational dataset (GRIDMET) were used as the reference for historical observations in this study. GRIDMET provides temperature data at a 4 km spatial resolution, available from 1979 up to the previous day. The dataset is developed by merging temporally rich data from the North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2 (NLDAS-2) with the spatially fine resolution (800-m) Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) data43. It was validated against an extensive network of automated weather stations across the western United States, showing skill comparable to station-based interpolation methods. For consistency with the model forecast period, we selected data from 1981 to 2024.Model data from the ECMWF, consisting of six-hourly forecasts, were averaged to obtain daily values for the November–December–January–February (NDJF) period. Forecasts were initialized on the first day of each month (November, December, January, and February) from 1981 to 2024 (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-original-single-levels?tab=download), including both 1-month lead forecasts (e.g., November initialized in November) and longer lead times up to 4 months depending on the initialization month. Thus, the lead time varied by forecast start date, ranging from near-zero (same-month initialization) to approximately 4 months in advance. Additionally, monthly mean forecasts for the NDJF season were obtained from ECMWF for the same years from one to four-month lead times (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-monthly-single-levels?tab=download). We chose ECMWF due to its widely recognized performance, as previous studies have established it as the gold standard for medium-range weather forecasting44.The ECMWF hindcasts are available at a spatial resolution of 1° × 1° (~111 km). However, at this coarse resolution, the model data are limited in their ability to capture temperature variations influenced by topography, proximity to coastlines, and other meso-climatic factors. In contrast, the observational dataset offers finer spatial detail and can represent these small-scale temperature variations more accurately, which is critical for realistic predictions of winter chill. To leverage the large-scale forecast signal while retaining fine-scale variability, we apply the ECMWF anomalies to the 4 km observational climatology using both mean and SD scaling. In this approach, the coarse-scale forecast anomaly is applied across the high-resolution grid, while the local variability from observations is explicitly preserved. This SD-ratio scaling improves chill estimation relative to using raw forecasts or mean-only bias correction. Our aim is not to predict exact daily chill at individual locations, but to capture anomalies in chill sufficiency at monthly-to-seasonal scales for crop-growing regions. This method has been successfully applied in previous studies16,45,46.To compute CP, hourly temperatures are required, which were derived from daily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures.                 In Eqs. (3) and (4), \({T}_{\max M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) refer to the daily maximum and minimum temperatures generated from ECMWF monthly forecasts at a spatial resolution of the observational data, 4 km for each lead time. \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) are the monthly anomalies of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF, calculated by subtracting their respective climatology from the ECMWF hindcasts. The terms \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) denote the monthly SDs of Tmax and Tmin from the ECMWF forecasts, while \({\sigma }_{{T}_{{{\mathrm{maxM}}}}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) represent the corresponding SDs from the reference data.Hourly temperatures needed for chill computation were derived from the daily Tmax and Tmin, latitude, and sunrise and sunset times. Daytime warming was modeled using a sine curve, while nighttime cooling was modeled with a logarithmic function, following the methods in the ChillR package developed by Luedeling et al47. To evaluate whether deriving hourly temperatures from daily Tmax and Tmin introduces substantial uncertainty in chill accumulation, we computed annual total CP using both methods for three representative stations (Modesto, Davis, and Five Points) using California Irrigation Management System (CIMIS) data (https://cimis.water.ca.gov/). The annual totals from these two approaches were computed using paired t-tests and Wilcoxon tests (implemented in R), which indicated no significant differences, confirming that the derived hourly temperatures provided a reliable estimate of chill accumulation.Chill quantificationChill units are a thermal-time metric quantifying exposure to effective chilling temperatures, and dormancy is completed once sufficient chill units have accumulated48. It can be measured as one hour at an optimal temperature, as in the Chilling Hours model, or a more complex unit of accumulated chill, as in the Dynamic Model. The total chilling requirement reflects the number of units or hours accumulated within a specified temperature range, which varies by species and cultivars49. Chill accumulation can be quantified using the Chilling Hours model, which counts the number of hours with temperatures between 0 °C and 7.2 °C50, while ignoring all other temperatures, or by calculating CP using the dynamic model1,3. The dynamic model is considered particularly well-suited for warming climates, such as that of California, because it represents chill accumulation in a more physiologically based manner than simpler chill-hour models and partially accounts for the reduction of chilling effectiveness at higher temperatures51,52,53.We quantified chill accumulation using CP, calculated with the Dynamic_model function from the ChillR package47. The dynamic model estimates CP through a two-step process: first, exposure to cold temperatures leads to the formation of an intermediate product, which can either be destroyed by subsequent warm temperatures or strengthened by moderate temperatures. Once a specific quantity of this intermediate product accumulates, it is permanently converted into a CP, unaffected by later temperature changes. The model needs hourly temperature data (in degrees Celsius) over a specified time period and applies experimentally derived constants within an exponential equation to compute the cumulative CP across the entire duration.We computed CP for the NDJF season total, as well as monthly total for the entire California using observational data, and ECMWF’s monthly and daily hindcasts for the period 1981–2024. For each season, chill accumulation from November and December of a given year was combined with January and February of the following year (e.g., November–December 1981 with January–February 1982). Chill computation of the 2024–2025 season was not possible, as January and February data for 2025 were unavailable at the time of this study.Analysis of CPWe computed the slope of the interannual total CP in the observational data between 1980 and 2023. For each grid point, the trend slope of total CP was estimated using Sen’s Slope Estimator20. This method computes the slope for each pair of data points as the ratio of the difference in chill values (y) to the difference in time (x). The final Sen’s Slope is the median of all pairwise slopes, providing a robust estimate of trend magnitude that is less sensitive to outliers or non-normal distributions than a simple least squares linear fit. The statistical significance of the trends (1980–2023) was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted Mann–Kendall test (Yue–Pilon modification)21,22. Both the trend slope and modified significance were computed in Python using the pymannkendall package.A trend was considered statistically significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. We then clipped the crop-growing regions on the gridded chill data, to determine the proportion of each region experiencing significant chill decline. The proportion of each region experiencing a significant decline was calculated by dividing the number of significant grid cells within the region by the total number of grid cells in that region, then multiplying by 100. We also calculated chill for Southern California (south of 37° latitude) and for individual counties by averaging chill values across all grid cells within the latitude band or county boundaries.We computed the statistical significance of the difference in the coefficient of variation (CV) of CP between the first (1980–1989) and last (2014–2023) decades. The CV was computed separately for each period, and a statistical comparison was conducted using an independent t-test54,55. The difference was considered significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. Additionally, we assessed the interannual variability by computing the SD of CP using a 10-year moving window for each crop-growing region.To examine whether CMIP6 projections capture the observed decline in chill accumulation, we calculated CP using Tmax and Tmin projections from Global Climate Models (GCMs) participating in Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6 (CMIP6) experiments for both historical (1981–2014) and future (2015–2100) periods for the entire California. We selected three GCMS — ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, EC-Earth3-Veg—based on their demonstrated effectiveness for the California region, as determined through process-based and statistical metric56. These models were also chosen because high-resolution (~3 km) statistically downscaled projections are available. These CMIP6 GCMs typically operate at coarse spatial resolutions, they do not adequately capture fine-scale geographic variation critical for accurate chill estimation. Therefore, we used downscaled outputs of these selected GCMs generated through the Localized Constructed Analogs, version 2 (LOCA2)57, which provide improved spatial resolution (3 km). The downscaled, gridded datasets for the period 2015–2100 are publicly available in NetCDF format through Cal-Adapt (https://cal-adapt.org/data/download/).For future projections, we used the high-emission scenario, Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP 5–8.5), which assumes continued growth in greenhouse gas emissions throughout the century58. Although multiple scenarios are commonly assessed in climate impact studies, we selected SSP 5–8.5 to enable a direct comparison with earlier winter chill assessments that used the highest emissions scenarios available at the time to establish the earliest plausible timeline of chill decline. Consistent with this objective, SSP 5–8.5 is used here to evaluate whether the upper-bound projected decline in winter chill is sufficient to explain the observed historical trends. Extending this analysis to multiple SSPs to assess scenario-dependent uncertainty remains an important direction for future work.We clipped these CMIP6 projected chill for each crop-growing region in Southern California and computed the average annual chill accumulation for each crop. We compared the number of years with insufficient chill in observed data and historical projections for the same period (1981–2014) to examine whether CMIP6 projections under- or overestimate the observed decline in chill. Also, we examined whether the continuous decline in chill is similar across all counties.The chill sufficiency thresholds for pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum were adopted from Pope5. For each crop, chilling requirements vary substantially across cultivars, locations, years, and experimental methods, particularly for stone fruits59. We use a crop-specific chill threshold defined as the median across all varieties of a given crop reported in Pope5. We use the median of CP, since it is less sensitive to extremes and enables consistent regional-scale comparisons in the absence of county-level varietal data. These thresholds are treated as representative benchmarks rather than fixed physiological limits. Consequently, our results are interpreted in terms of increasing frequency and proximity of episodic chill insufficiency driven by interannual variability, rather than the precise timing of persistent threshold crossings inferred from long-term trends. While cultivars with higher-than-median chill requirements may face greater vulnerability and lower-chill cultivars may experience reduced risk, our objective is not to predict cultivar-specific outcomes but to characterize regional-scale chill risk and its temporal evolution under observed conditions, climate projections, and forecasts.To evaluate interannual predictive performance, we compared how many years in the observational record were correctly captured by ECMWF forecasts. Forecast accuracy was evaluated at two thresholds: predictions were considered correct if the predicted CP were within 10% or within 5% of the observed NDJF totals, since large overestimates could misrepresent chill sufficiency. Although crops may tolerate up to a 20% shortfall, we adopted this conservative threshold to offer more robust forecasts of chill conditions25.We evaluated the accuracy of chill forecasts derived from bias-corrected ECMWF hindcasts initialized on the first day of each month (November through February), using both daily and monthly mean products. The analysis included forecasts with lead times ranging from 1 to 4 months, depending on initialization month. Forecast skill in predicting chill was assessed at a shorter 1-month lead, with February forecasts first, followed by comparing longer lead time forecasts.We performed this analysis at the crop-growing region level in Southern California (<37° latitude) by clipping the chill data to each region and calculating regional averages. Also, we performed the analysis at the individual grid level. Additionally, we calculated the ACC for each grid between the ECMWF-derived chill and that computed from observations.We also assessed the extent to which interannual variability in winter chill accumulation is influenced by the El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO). To do this, we used the ONI from NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center (CPC), which is based on three-month rolling averages of sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies in the Niño 3.4 region (5° N–5° S, 120°–170° W), derived from the Extended Reconstructed SST dataset, version 5 (ERSST.v5) (https://origin.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/analysis_monitoring/ensostuff/ONI_v5.php). We then computed both lead and lag correlations between the Niño 3.4 index and CP to evaluate the temporal relationship between ENSO phases and chill variability.

Data availability
              
              We used several publicly available datasets in this study. GRIDMET meteorological data are available from the Northwest Knowledge Network (https://www.northwestknowledge.net/metdata/data/). CMIP6 downscaled climate projections (LOCA2) were obtained via the Intake ESM catalog (https://cadcat.s3.amazonaws.com/cae-collection.json). Crop distribution data were obtained from the USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Cropland Data Layer (https://croplandcros.scinet.usda.gov/) as GeoTIFF files and processed into crop-specific spatial masks using ArcGIS Pro. ECMWF seasonal forecast data (daily and monthly products) were obtained from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). All climate and crop datasets were spatially aligned on a common latitude–longitude grid (WGS84), and crop-specific grid cells were used to extract climate variables for subsequent analysis. Processed datasets used to generate the figures are publicly available on Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20058559. Figure-specific datasets include GRIDMET-derived chill time series, CMIP6 historical and SSP585 projections, ECMWF bias-corrected and monthly-derived chill products, and crop distribution and location datasets used for spatial aggregation and visualization.
            Code availability
              
              All chill computations were performed in R (v4.3.3) using open-source geospatial and climate analysis libraries. Specifically, chill calculations for GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 datasets were implemented using the chillR package, applying the Dynamic Model after conversion of daily temperature inputs to hourly temperature using chillR::make_hourly_temps. NetCDF handling and supporting data processing steps within R were performed using the ncdf4, terra, dplyr, and tidyr packages. R workflows were parallelized using furrr and future for efficient grid-cell-level computation. All other data processing, geospatial workflows, and analysis pipelines were implemented in Python (v3.11.11). This included GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 preprocessing, spatial mapping, observational–model comparison workflows, crop-specific time series computation, and statistical analyses. Python-based workflows relied on open-source packages including NumPy, Pandas, GeoPandas, Xarray, SciPy, Matplotlib, and Shapely. All model-specific preprocessing steps are implemented in the scripts corresponding to each analysis component. Final integration of R-derived chill outputs and Python-processed datasets was used to generate all reported results, including regional (south of 37° N) and grid-level performance metrics. All scripts were developed in-house and follow a fully reproducible workflow using consistent input datasets. All code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/pkj002/califo_work/tree/main/CommSust.

items.description: La legge sulla valorizzazione del mare contiene una norma di principio per la tutela di un ecosistema unico, ma per ora non sono previsti strumenti re
items.body:
Rigenerare le praterie di Posidonia oceanica nel mar Mediterraneo, con la Water Defenders Alliance

Per salvare i nostri mari, cambiamo prospettiva

Marine animal forests, il documentario che ci porta sotto la superficie, del mare e delle notizie
		
		
			Ecosistemi complessi, le marine animal forests forniscono servizi essenziali per la sopravvivenza di molte specie, inclusa la nostra.

				di Chiara Boracchi

La prima nave da crociera a idrogeno al mondo è italiana. Salperà nel 2026
		
		
			Fincantieri e Viking annunciano la prima nave da crociera a idrogeno al mondo, un’innovazione italiana nel settore della navigazione che potrebbe ridefinire gli standard ambientali del turismo marittimo.

				di Roberto Sposini

Le 487 spiagge che hanno ottenuto la Bandiera blu 2025 in Italia
		
		
			È aumentato il numero di Comuni italiani sui cui territori sono presenti spiagge alle quali è stata riconosciuta la Bandiera blu.
		
		
			 Ambiente
			14 maggio 2025
			
				di Andrea Barolini

Cosa c’è nei fondali del Mediterraneo? Forse non volete saperlo
			
			
				Un’immersione a oltre 5.000 metri di profondità ha svelato un fondale pieno di plastica e rifiuti: la Calypso deep, nel cuore del Mediterraneo, si conferma uno dei luoghi più inquinati degli abissi marini.
